rule: Edges represent planned semantic relationships between terms. No edge may be rendered as a factual claim in production without source validation.

edges.explanation: Bisulfid (German) and bisulfide (English) are separated by the single letter E removed under German chemical nomenclature conventions. This spelling boundary is the conceptual foundation of bisulfid.com.
Bisulfid and Sulfid share the German chemical compression pattern: English -ide endings become German -id endings. Sulfide to Sulfid parallels Bisulfide to Bisulfid.
Bisulfide is a member of the broader sulfide chemical family. The exact chemical relationship requires source validation before assertion.
Bisulfide and hydrosulfide are related chemical terms. Whether they are synonymous in some contexts requires source validation.
Sodium bisulfide is a key industrial compound in the bisulfide chemical family, used in mining, water treatment, and other industrial applications.
Sodium bisulfide and sodium hydrosulfide are related names for NaHS. Whether they refer to the same compound and the naming conventions require source validation.
Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is chemically related to bisulfide chemistry but has a distinct and acute hazard profile. The safety context must not conflate bisulfide safety with H2S safety.
Bisulfide and bisulfite are distinct chemical species. Bisulfite data must never be used as bisulfide data. This edge exists to enforce that boundary.
Bisulfid (German form) refers to bisulfide, not bisulfite. This edge reinforces the disambiguation at the center node level.
Disulfide is a related structural class within sulfide chemistry, with relevance to advanced materials, biochemistry, and future materials research.
Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a key industrial and advanced materials compound in the disulfide family, used in lubricants and electronics.
Sulfid (German) and sulfide (English) represent the same German-English chemical compression boundary as bisulfid and bisulfide. This edge supports the asset's core linguistic argument.
